tasks.prompt: [Warm-up] Install ArgoCD in your cluster and access the UI. After installation, verify the Pod status in the argocd namespace, then expose argocd-server via NodePort or port-forward and log in with the initial admin password.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Install ArgoCD
kubectl create namespace argocd
kubectl apply -n argocd -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

# 2. Wait for all Pods to reach Running state
kubectl get pods -n argocd -w

# 3. Expose argocd-server locally via port-forward
kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd ___:443
# Access https://localhost:<port>

# 4. Retrieve the initial admin password
kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

# 5. Log in via argocd CLI (optional)
argocd login localhost:___  --username admin --password <password above> --insecure
tasks.solution:
# 1~2. Installation (run the commands above)

# 3. port-forward (run in background or a separate terminal)
kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443 &
# Access https://localhost:8080 in a browser

# 4. Initial admin password
kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret \
  -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
# Example output: XkYjPqR4mN2vL8wZ  (different every installation)

# 5. CLI login
argocd login localhost:8080 --username admin --password $(kubectl -n argocd get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d) --insecure
# 'admin:login' logged in successfully

# 6. Verify connected cluster
argocd cluster list
# SERVER                                  NAME        STATUS
# https://kubernetes.default.svc          in-cluster  Successful
tasks.explanation: ArgoCD is a Kubernetes-native GitOps tool that uses a Git repository as the single source of truth for cluster state. Installation creates several components: argocd-server (API/UI), argocd-repo-server (Git integration), argocd-application-controller (sync controller), and argocd-dex-server (authentication). Port-forward is a quick way to access a service in a local development environment. In production, use an Ingress or LoadBalancer Service instead.
hints: If port-forward fails with 'bind: address already in use', try a different port (e.g., 9090).
All operations are available through the browser UI without installing the argocd CLI. The CLI is useful for automation and scripting.
If the initial admin password Secret is missing, find the argocd-server Pod name and run: kubectl -n argocd exec -it argocd-server-xxx -- argocd admin initial-password

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Create an ArgoCD Application pointing to Kubernetes manifests in a public GitHub repository (or a local Git server). Set the sync policy to 'manual' and perform the first manual sync to verify the application is deployed to the cluster.
tasks.starter:
# Using the official ArgoCD example repository
# https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps
# Path: guestbook/ (Deployment + Service)

# Write the Application manifest
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: guestbook
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git
    targetRevision: ___   # HEAD or a specific branch/tag/commit
    path: ___             # path to manifests in the repository (guestbook)
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: ___        # target namespace for deployment
  syncPolicy:
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
    # no 'automated' here (manual sync)

# Apply
kubectl apply -f guestbook-app.yaml

# Check Application status
kubectl get applications -n argocd

# Perform manual sync
argocd app sync ___
tasks.solution:
# Create the Application
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: guestbook
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: guestbook
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: guestbook
  syncPolicy:
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
EOF
# application.argoproj.io/guestbook created

# Check status (OutOfSync before sync)
kubectl get applications -n argocd
# NAME        SYNC STATUS   HEALTH STATUS
# guestbook   OutOfSync     Missing

# Perform manual sync
argocd app sync guestbook
# Or with kubectl:
# kubectl patch application guestbook -n argocd --type=merge -p '{"operation":{"sync":{}}}'

# Status after sync
kubectl get applications -n argocd
# NAME        SYNC STATUS   HEALTH STATUS
# guestbook   Synced        Healthy

# Verify deployed resources
kubectl get all -n guestbook
tasks.explanation: An ArgoCD Application declares which path in a Git repository to deploy to which namespace in the cluster. Without syncPolicy.automated, the Application operates in manual sync mode. OutOfSync means the Git source and cluster state differ; Synced means they match. Health status reflects whether the actual resources (Deployment, Pods, etc.) are operating normally. In GitOps, a Git commit — not a kubectl apply — is the trigger for deployment.
hints: If GitHub is not accessible, clone the repository and use a file:// URL as the local path, or use a local Gitea server.
Clicking the 'Sync' button in the UI has the same effect as argocd app sync.
If Health Status shows Degraded, check kubectl get pods -n guestbook for Pod status first.

tasks.prompt: [Core Practice] Practice ArgoCD self-healing and drift detection. Enable automated syncPolicy (selfHeal: true, prune: true) on the Application, then manually change a resource in the cluster to introduce drift. Observe ArgoCD automatically reverting the cluster back to the Git state.
tasks.solution:
# 1. Enable selfHeal + prune
kubectl patch application guestbook -n argocd \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"spec":{"syncPolicy":{"automated":{"selfHeal":true,"prune":true}}}}'

# 2. Introduce drift (force replicas to 5)
kubectl scale deployment guestbook-ui -n guestbook --replicas=5

# 3. Watch auto-recovery (within seconds to tens of seconds)
kubectl get deployment guestbook-ui -n guestbook -w
# NAME           READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
# guestbook-ui   5/5     5            5           5m   <-- briefly 5
# guestbook-ui   1/1     1            1           5m   <-- ArgoCD restores Git value (1)

# 4. Application status
argocd app get guestbook
# Sync Status: Synced
# Health Status: Healthy

# 5. Sync history
argocd app history guestbook
# ID   DATE                           REVISION
# 0    2024-01-01 00:00:00 +0000 UTC  HEAD (xxxx)
# 1    2024-01-01 00:01:00 +0000 UTC  HEAD (xxxx)   <-- self-heal sync

# 6. Test prune: create an extra resource directly
kubectl create configmap extra-config -n guestbook --from-literal=key=val
# After a few seconds ArgoCD auto-deletes it (not in Git)
kubectl get configmap -n guestbook
tasks.explanation: With selfHeal: true, ArgoCD compares cluster state against Git state every 3 minutes (default) and automatically synchronizes any drift it finds. Changing replicas via kubectl scale is reverted by ArgoCD back to the Git value. prune: true removes resources from the cluster that no longer exist in Git. Together these two settings implement the GitOps guarantee that 'Git is the sole source of truth for the cluster.' All changes must now flow through a Git commit.
hints: The default selfHeal check interval is 3 minutes (180 seconds). Adjust it with the timeout.reconciliation key in the argocd-cm ConfigMap.
prune: true is a powerful setting. When enabling it in production for the first time, use ArgoCD UI's 'Diff' feature to review resources that will be deleted.
If you want immediate sync on drift detection, check the 'server.disable.auth' setting in the argocd-cmd-params-cm ConfigMap.

tasks.prompt: [Application] Practice deploying an ArgoCD Application across multiple environments (dev/prod). Create two Applications pointing to different paths (or branches) in the same Git repository. After pushing a manifest change to the dev path in Git, confirm that only the dev Application becomes OutOfSync.
tasks.starter:
# Prerequisite: fork argocd-example-apps or use a local Git server
# Here we simulate with different paths

# dev Application (guestbook path)
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: guestbook-dev
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: ___          # dev environment path
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: guestbook-dev
  syncPolicy:
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
    automated:
      selfHeal: true

---
# prod Application (helm-guestbook path — simulates a different deployment unit)
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: guestbook-prod
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: ___          # prod environment path
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: guestbook-prod
  syncPolicy:
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
    # no automated (prod requires manual approval)

# Check both Applications simultaneously
kubectl get applications -n argocd
tasks.solution:
# Create both Applications
kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: guestbook-dev
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: guestbook
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: guestbook-dev
  syncPolicy:
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
    automated:
      selfHeal: true
EOF

kubectl apply -f - <<'EOF'
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: guestbook-prod
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/argoproj/argocd-example-apps.git
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: helm-guestbook
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: guestbook-prod
  syncPolicy:
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
EOF

# Sync both Applications
argocd app sync guestbook-dev
argocd app sync guestbook-prod

# Check status
kubectl get applications -n argocd
# NAME             SYNC STATUS   HEALTH STATUS
# guestbook-dev    Synced        Healthy
# guestbook-prod   Synced        Healthy

# Drift on dev only: guestbook-dev reverts via selfHeal
# prod has no automated sync, so it stays OutOfSync
kubectl scale deployment -n guestbook-prod --replicas=3 --all
kubectl get applications -n argocd
# NAME             SYNC STATUS   HEALTH STATUS
# guestbook-dev    Synced        Healthy
# guestbook-prod   OutOfSync     Healthy  <-- prod requires manual sync

# Clean up
argocd app delete guestbook guestbook-dev guestbook-prod
kubectl delete namespace guestbook guestbook-dev guestbook-prod
tasks.explanation: In a multi-environment (dev/prod) GitOps pattern, dev typically uses automated selfHeal for rapid feedback, while prod uses manual sync to require a review and approval step before any change takes effect. Using ArgoCD's App of Apps pattern or ApplicationSet allows you to manage dozens of environments and clusters as code. Separating environments by directory structure (or branch) in a Git repository enables declarative management of multiple environments from a single ArgoCD instance.
hints: The helm-guestbook path is the same app packaged as a Helm chart. ArgoCD supports plain YAML, Helm, and Kustomize natively.
In real teams, it is recommended to restrict sync permissions on the prod Application to specific roles using ArgoCD RBAC.
Use argocd app diff <name> to preview the diff between Git and the cluster before syncing.
